label: National Palace — AMLO Headquarters
sub: AMLO relocated the presidency from Los Pinos to the historic National Palace on the Zócalo on Day 1. The daily mañanera press conference was held here every weekday for six years (1,833 total briefings).

label: Texcoco — NAIM Airport Cancelled
sub: Site of the canceled New International Airport of Mexico City (NAIM), a $13.3B Peña Nieto project that was 30% complete when AMLO held a referendum and cancelled it in December 2018. A 2021 audit found the cancellation cost 184.55 billion pesos.

label: AIFA — Felipe Ángeles International Airport
sub: Built by the Army at the former Santa Lucía military airbase in Zumpango, Estado de México. Inaugurated March 21, 2022 after 886 days of construction. Official cost: 74.5 billion pesos; independent analysis found actual cost ~116 billion pesos.

label: Culiacán — Culiacanazo (Oct 2019)
sub: Site of the October 17, 2019 'Culiacanazo': 700 armed Sinaloa Cartel gunmen paralyzed the city after security forces briefly captured Ovidio Guzmán. AMLO ordered his release to end the standoff. Became the defining symbol of 'abrazos no balazos' doctrine's limits.

label: Culiacán — Ovidio Capture (Jan 2023)
sub: On January 5, 2023, Mexican Army forces captured Ovidio Guzmán López (son of El Chapo) in the Jesús María neighborhood of Culiacán. The Sinaloa Cartel retaliated with unprecedented force, shutting down the city, shooting down a military helicopter, and killing 10 soldiers.

label: Dos Bocas — Olmeca Refinery (Tabasco)
sub: The Olmeca Refinery in Paraíso, Tabasco — Mexico's first new refinery since the 1970s. Original budget ~$8B USD; final cost exceeded $20B USD. Operating at only 17% capacity as of December 2024. Groundbreaking June 2, 2019.

label: Palenque — Tren Maya Western Terminus
sub: Western terminus of the Tren Maya railway, near the Palenque Maya archaeological site in Chiapas. AMLO retired to his ranch in Palenque, Chiapas after leaving office in October 2024.

label: Cancún — Tren Maya Eastern Hub
sub: Cancún Airport is a key station on the Tren Maya network (Sections 2–4, inaugurated Dec 2023). The railway was designed to connect tourism hubs across the Yucatán Peninsula. Average ridership in the first seven months post-inauguration was only ~1,200 passengers per day.

label: Mérida — Tren Maya Central Hub
sub: Capital of Yucatán state and central hub of the Tren Maya network. Mérida station connects the peninsula's largest city to Cancún, Valladolid, and Chichén Itzá via the railway. Station area construction displaced local communities near historic sites.

label: Campeche — Tren Maya Inauguration Site
sub: AMLO led the inaugural run ceremony in Campeche city on December 15, 2023, unveiling a plaque to mark the opening of the Tren Maya. Section 1 of the railway (Palenque to Escárcega) runs through Campeche state.

label: Tulum — Tren Maya Southern Quintana Roo
sub: Tulum station on the Tren Maya line runs through a section that required tunneling through sensitive karst terrain and protected cenote systems. Environmental groups documented damage to the Great Maya Aquifer. Station opened with passenger service in December 2023.

label: Tijuana — High-Crime Border City
sub: Tijuana consistently ranked among the world's most violent cities during AMLO's presidency, with cartel territorial disputes driving homicide rates exceeding 100 per 100,000. The Tijuana-San Diego border crossing was a key fentanyl trafficking corridor that strained US-Mexico relations throughout the sexenio.

label: Guadalajara — CJNG Cartel Stronghold
sub: Capital of Jalisco state and home base of the Jalisco New Generation Cartel (CJNG), which expanded dramatically during AMLO's presidency, contesting territories in 28 of 32 states. The CJNG's rise under 'abrazos no balazos' represented a major challenge to national security policy.

label: Celaya, Guanajuato — Cartel Battleground
sub: Celaya in Guanajuato became one of Mexico's most violent cities as CJNG fought the Santa Rosa de Lima Cartel for fuel theft (huachicoleo) routes and territory. Guanajuato had the highest homicide rate of any state during AMLO's presidency, defying the national downward trend.

label: Veracruz — Gulf of Mexico Port
sub: Mexico's largest port and key for hydrocarbon exports. AMLO's energy nationalism policies aimed to expand Pemex's refinery capacity (including Dos Bocas) to reduce crude oil exports and increase refined product production. The port also saw increased military customs oversight under AMLO's securitization of infrastructure.

label: Monterrey — Nearshoring Investment Hub
sub: Mexico's industrial capital, Monterrey in Nuevo León became the epicenter of the nearshoring boom as US-China trade tensions drove manufacturers to relocate to Mexico. In 2023, Mexico overtook China and Canada as the US's top trading partner. Monterrey saw record industrial real estate investment and manufacturing expansion in automotive, electronics, and medical devices sectors.

label: Oaxaca — AMLO's Political Heartland
sub: Oaxaca is the cultural heartland of AMLO's political base. Several of his closest allies and cabinet members hail from Oaxaca, including Adán Augusto López (who was born in Tabasco but linked to Oaxaca politics). The state is home to large indigenous communities whose rights and land were contested by Tren Maya construction plans.

label: Salina Cruz — Interoceanic Corridor Pacific Port
sub: Pacific terminus of the Corredor Interoceánico del Istmo de Tehuantepec, inaugurated September 2023. The 303 km rail corridor connects this Gulf of Tehuantepec port to Coatzacoalcos on the Gulf of Mexico, positioned as a land bridge alternative to the Panama Canal.

label: Coatzacoalcos — Interoceanic Corridor Gulf Port
sub: Gulf of Mexico terminus of the Istmo de Tehuantepec Interoceanic Corridor in Veracruz state. The corridor also passes through Oaxaca, connecting 10 planned Special Economic Zones (ZEDEs) along the 303 km route intended to attract manufacturing and industrial investment.

label: Ciudad Juárez — Northern Border Violence
sub: El Paso–Ciudad Juárez metropolitan border crossing, one of the world's busiest. Under AMLO, Chihuahua state including Juárez experienced persistent cartel violence related to fentanyl and methamphetamine trafficking. Femicide rates remained high; women's rights activists repeatedly criticized AMLO's dismissive responses to gender-based killings.

label: Los Pinos — Opened as Cultural Center
sub: Los Pinos, the official presidential residence since 1934, was converted by AMLO into a public cultural center on his first day in office (December 1, 2018). The gesture symbolized his rejection of presidential privilege. The complex in Chapultepec forest now hosts museums, exhibitions, and public events.

label: Puebla — Morena Electoral Test State
sub: Puebla was an important Morena electoral battleground. Governor Claudia Rivera (Morena) replaced the earlier PAN-allied governor after 2018. The state hosted significant infrastructure projects including road improvements and the completion of the Mexico City-Puebla highway expansion under AMLO's government.

label: Chichén Itzá — Tren Maya Heritage Site Station
sub: UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of Mexico's most visited archaeological sites. A Tren Maya station was built near Chichén Itzá as part of the railway's cultural tourism mission. Archaeologists documented construction damage to protected subsurface artifacts and cave systems in the vicinity.

label: Villahermosa, Tabasco — AMLO's Home State Capital
sub: Villahermosa is the capital of Tabasco, AMLO's home state and a stronghold of his political movement. Tabasco was one of the primary beneficiaries of AMLO's infrastructure projects: both the Dos Bocas Olmeca Refinery (Paraíso) and the western Tren Maya sections run through the state. AMLO's ally Adán Augusto López served as Tabasco governor (2019–2021).

label: Palenque — AMLO's Retirement Ranch
sub: AMLO retired to his ranch near Palenque, Chiapas after handing power to Claudia Sheinbaum on October 1, 2024. He had pledged to leave public life after his term. The Tren Maya's western terminus also runs through Palenque, making the town one of the megaproject's showcases.

label: Nuevo Laredo — CDN Cartel Violence
sub: Nuevo Laredo across from Laredo, Texas was a center of operations for the Cártel del Noreste (CDN), which fought bloody territorial battles with the military and rival organizations throughout AMLO's term. Attacks on soldiers, police, and journalists were documented in 2019–2023.

label: Hermosillo, Sonora — Deadliest State for Journalists
sub: Hermosillo is the capital of Sonora, which had the highest count of journalist killings under AMLO (7 cases in 2022 alone). The state borders Arizona and sees heavy cartel activity related to drug and migrant smuggling. Mexican army deployments increased across the state as part of the National Guard buildup.

label: Toluca — Interurban Rail Project
sub: The Mexico City–Toluca Interurban Train (El Insurgente) was an inherited Peña Nieto project that AMLO completed in stages. Phase 1 (Toluca metro area stations) opened September 2023; Phase 2 (to Santa Fe, Mexico City) opened August 31, 2024. Full completion to Observatorio metro station occurred February 2026. Final cost reached 73.72 billion pesos vs. the 42.72 billion pesos original estimate.

label: Chetumal — Tren Maya Southern Terminus
sub: Chetumal, the capital of Quintana Roo on the Belize border, is the southern terminus of the Tren Maya. The final section (Escárcega to Chetumal, sections 6–7) was inaugurated by President Sheinbaum on December 15, 2024, completing the entire 1,554 km railway loop. The Chetumal–Cancún leg passes through heavily contested environmental and indigenous territory.

label: Chiapas — Tren Maya Indigenous Territory Impact
sub: Communities in Chiapas near the Agua Azul waterfalls and Lacandon jungle were among the indigenous groups challenging the Tren Maya's legitimacy. The UN Human Rights Council and OHCHR stated the December 2019 indigenous consultation did not comply with international standards for free, prior, and informed consent. Multiple court injunctions were filed and dismissed in 2020–2022.

label: Mexico City — CFE Headquarters
sub: Headquarters of the Comisión Federal de Electricidad (CFE), the state electricity utility at the center of AMLO's energy nationalism. Under CFE Director Manuel Bartlett, the utility reversed course on renewable energy auctions, prioritized dispatch from aging thermal plants, and pursued a constitutional electricity reform (defeated April 2022). The USMCA dispute launched in June 2022 centered on CFE's discriminatory dispatch practices.
